Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty chaotic but I made it work. I'd listen to lecture recordings and podcasts while commuting or doing chores since I learn best through audio. With limited resources at home, I couldn't always study in ideal conditions, so I'd find pockets of time throughout the day rather than having a set schedule. I probably spent around 23 hours a week studying, but it was scattered across different times depending on what else was going on in my life.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot based on deadlines and my work schedule. I have to balance a lot of responsibilities because my family doesn't have much, so sometimes I'd cram before exams and other times I'd have a few days where I could study more consistently. Deadlines definitely drove when I'd put in the most intense effort. I wish I could have been more regular, but life just doesn't allow for that kind of predictability when you're managing limited resources.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I knew there was something specific I needed to check—like assignment due dates, new lecture uploads, or to participate in discussions for marks. Sometimes the gamified tasks would send me notifications and that would pull me back in. I also logged in when I felt anxious about falling behind, which happened more often than I'd like to admit. The fear of missing something important was a big motivator.
Q4: Yeah, after a few weeks, checking the LMS became almost automatic, like checking my phone for messages. The gamified elements helped with that—earning points or badges gave me small wins that kept me coming back. I'd log in during breaks or before bed just to see if anything new was posted. It became part of my daily rhythm even when I wasn't actively studying.
Q5: The video lectures were essential for me since I'm an auditory learner—I'd listen to them multiple times. Discussion forums were also valuable because hearing other students' perspectives helped concepts click in ways that just reading couldn't. The gamified tasks made studying feel less like a chore and more like something I could actually accomplish, which helped with my anxiety about the course.
Q6: I tended to skip the reading materials and PDFs because I just don't absorb information well that way. If it wasn't audio-based, I'd often delay engaging with it until absolutely necessary. Some of the peer interaction features I didn't use much because I felt awkward reaching out to people I didn't know. I know I only had like 2 peer interactions logged, and honestly that's because I was anxious about how I'd come across.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to exam performance or if it helped reduce my anxiety about understanding the material. The gamified tasks were great because they gave immediate feedback and made me feel like I was making progress. If something felt like busywork or didn't clearly connect to what would be assessed, I'd prioritize other things instead.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford time-wise. Required ones I'd push through even when I was exhausted or anxious, but optional ones I'd only do if I genuinely found them interesting or if I had extra time, which was rare. There was less pressure with optional stuff, but also less motivation to complete them when I was already stretched thin with everything else.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my studying happened outside the LMS. I'd download lecture audio and listen to it on my phone during my commute or while working. I used YouTube videos to supplement concepts that weren't clicking, and I'd record myself summarizing material and play it back. A lot of my learning happened through these methods that wouldn't show up in any system logs.
Q10: There were many times I'd be studying intensely but the LMS wouldn't capture it. I'd listen to downloaded lectures for hours while doing other tasks—that's invisible to the system. I'd also discuss concepts with a cousin who's in a similar field, talking through problems out loud. My actual study time was way more than what my attendance or login patterns would suggest.
Q11: All the time. I'd watch a video once online, download the audio, and then replay it multiple times offline over the following days. With my internet being unreliable sometimes and needing to study during commutes, downloading once and studying offline was my main strategy. The LMS would only show that initial access, not the hours I spent with that material afterward.
Q12: There were definitely periods when my anxiety spiked and it became really hard to engage. When assignments piled up or when I was worried about money stuff at home, I'd freeze up and avoid logging in because it felt overwhelming. My attendance dropped to really low levels during those times. It wasn't that I didn't care—I cared too much and it paralyzed me.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from working or dealing with family responsibilities, I'd log in but not really absorb anything. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those modules entirely for a while because facing them triggered my anxiety. Sometimes I'd have competing deadlines from different courses and this one would get pushed aside temporarily. My LMS usage was very much affected by what else was happening in my life.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd be scrolling through materials but my mind was elsewhere, worrying about other things. I'd watch videos but realize I hadn't actually processed anything because I was too anxious or tired. Those sessions would show up as engagement in the system, but they weren't productive at all. It's like being physically present but mentally checked out.
Q15: I postponed assignments a lot, which is why my completion rate was only around 51%. Even when I knew something was important, the anxiety would make me avoid starting because I was scared of failing or not doing well enough. I'd tell myself I'd do it later when I felt more prepared, but that feeling rarely came. It's a cycle I'm still trying to break.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that could override my avoidance patterns. I'd have huge spikes in activity right before things were due, cramming and submitting at the last minute. Without deadlines, I'd drift and let anxiety keep me from engaging. They created pressure that was uncomfortable but necessary for me to actually complete things.
Q17: Feedback was really important for my motivation. When I got positive feedback, it reduced my anxiety and made me want to engage more. But if feedback was delayed or critical, I'd sometimes spiral and avoid the course for a bit. The gamified elements gave instant feedback which was perfect for someone like me who needs that immediate reassurance.
Q18: The course structure influenced me a lot. Having clear modules and knowing exactly what was expected helped manage my anxiety. But when things were vague or when there were too many components to track, I'd get overwhelmed. The gamified tasks gave structure to my engagement in a way that felt manageable and even enjoyable.
Q19: The gamified elements definitely made engagement easier—they broke things into smaller, achievable goals which helped with my anxiety. What made it harder was when there were long readings or when participation required putting myself out there in discussions. Having audio options for content would have helped me a lot more given how I learn best.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me who logs in frequently out of anxiety, checking and rechecking things, but then struggles to actually submit work because of fear of failure or perfectionism. High activity might also mean someone is trying hard but confused about what's actually required. Or they might be engaging deeply with content but in ways that don't result in traditional submissions.
Q21: That's basically my story. My attendance was only 11% and my assignment completion was around half, but I scored nearly 98 on exams and got a Distinction. I studied extensively offline through audio materials and my own methods. The LMS activity doesn't capture how I actually learned—it just shows the tip of the iceberg.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active and what you click on, but they miss the quality of engagement entirely. They can't tell if you're anxiously scrolling versus deeply learning. They miss all offline studying, discussions with others outside the platform, and the mental effort you're putting in. For auditory learners like me who download and listen elsewhere, logs are especially misleading.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. It's when I'm actively thinking about concepts, connecting them to other things I know, and genuinely trying to master something rather than just pass. For me, engagement often happens in my head while I'm listening to lectures on repeat, not necessarily while I'm logged into a system.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled with anxiety throughout the course, or how hard I had to work to overcome limited resources. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent listening to downloaded lectures or the conversations with family that helped me understand concepts. It also wouldn't show that despite my low attendance and incomplete assignments, I was deeply committed to actually learning the material—which is why I performed well on exams.
